title: Case 91804: College Decision 047/26/COL - Norway's derogation under Art. 49(5) of the Gas Directive
text:
Case No: 91804
Document No: 1582842
Decision No: 047/26/COL
EFTA SURVEILLANCE AUTHORITY DECISION
of 27 February 2026
on the temporary derogation for the Norwegian gas distribution system operator Lyse
Neo AS from third-party access requirements under Directive 2009/73/EC of 13 July
2009 concerning common rules for the internal market in natural gas
THE EFTA SURVEILLANCE AUTHORITY,
Having regard to:
the Agreement on the European Economic Area1 (‘the EEA Agreement’), in particular Article
24 and Annex IV thereof,
the Act referred to at point 23 of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement:
Directive 2009/73/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 July 2009
concerning common rules for the internal market in natural gas and repealing Directive
2003/55/EC (‘the Gas Directive’),2 and in particular Articles 49(4) and 49(5) thereof, as
adapted to the EEA Agreement,3
Whereas:
1 INTRODUCTION
(1) Norway is part of the internal natural gas market through the EEA Agreement. The
objective of the EEA Agreement is to create a homogeneous European Economic Area
(‘EEA’). Relevant EU legislation for the internal natural gas market is incorporated into the
EEA Agreement so that it applies throughout the whole EEA, ensuring uniform application
of laws relating to this market and thus a level playing field throughout the EEA.
(2) Following the incorporation of the Gas Directive into Annex IV to the EEA Agreement by
Decision of the EEA Joint Committee No. 93/2017 of 5 May 2017 (‘JCD No. 93/2017’),4
the requirements of the Gas Directive, as adapted to the EEA Agreement, apply to
Norway.
1 OJ L 1, 3.1.1994, p. 3.
2 OJ L 211, 14.8.2009, p. 94.
3 EEA Joint Committee Decision No. 93/2017 of 5 May 2017, in particular the adaptation set out at point
23(l) of Annex IV to the EEA Agreement.
4 EEA Joint Committee Decision No. 93/2017 of 5 May 2017 entered into force on 3 October 2019.
Avenue des Arts 19H, 1000 Brussels, tel: +32 2 286 18 11, www.eftasurv.int

Page 2
(3) EEA States are required pursuant to Article 32 of the Gas Directive to ensure objective
and non-discriminatory third-party access to the transmission and distribution systems.
(4) Articles 49(4) and 49(5) of the Gas Directive, as adapted by JCD No. 93/2017, allow,
under certain conditions, a temporary derogation to be granted from, inter alia, the third-
party access requirements where the implementation of the Gas Directive would cause
substantial problems in a geographically limited area of an EEA State, “in particular
concerning the development of the transmission and major distribution infrastructure, and
with a view to encouraging investments”.
(5) Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive, as adapted by JCD No. 93/2017, exempts two
geographically limited areas in Norway from, inter alia, Article 32 of the Gas Directive: i)
Jæren and Ryfylke, and ii) Hordaland. The derogation applies for a maximum of 20 years
after the entry into force of JCD No. 93/2017, subject to a decision by the Norwegian
energy regulatory authority (‘RME’) every five years on the need to continue the
derogation. RME is to notify the EEA Joint Committee and the EFTA Surveillance
Authority (‘the Authority’) of its decision and the assessment it is based upon.
(6) Within a period of two months from the day following the receipt of RME’s decision,5 the
Authority may adopt a decision requiring RME to amend or withdraw its decision. In such
a case, RME is to comply with the Authority’s decision within a period of one month and
to inform the EEA Joint Committee and the Authority accordingly.
(7) The present Decision follows from RME’s notification to the Authority on 3 November
2025 of its decision of the same date,6 by which RME prolonged the derogation granted
to the Norwegian gas distribution system operator (‘DSO’) in Jæren and Ryfylke, Lyse
Neo AS (‘Lyse Neo’), from the third-party access requirements under Article 32 of the Gas
Directive7 for a period of five years from 1 January 2026 (‘the RME Decision’).
(8) The RME Decision was issued following the previous decision notified by RME to the
Authority on 3 October 2024,8 which continued the derogation for Lyse Neo from third-
party access requirements under Article 32 of the Gas Directive for a shorter period from
3 October 2024 until 31 December 2025. The Authority, having identified a lack of
information and analysis to demonstrate the need for a continued derogation, issued a
decision on 19 March 2025 (‘the Authority’s Decision No. 025/25/COL’),9 which required
RME to amend its decision of 3 October 2024 by providing certain clarifications and
justifications.10 RME subsequently adopted an amending decision on 22 April 2025.11
(9) This Decision is issued following the Authority’s review of the RME Decision.
5 This period may be extended with the consent of both the Authority and RME.
6 The date of the RME Decision was corrected from 27 October 2025 to 3 November 2025 by email
dated 18 February 2025.
7 Reference is made in the RME Decision to the Norwegian provisions implementing Article 32 of the
Gas Directive, namely Section 5-1 and Chapters 3,6 and 7 of the Norwegian regulation on natural gas
no. 1342 of 14 November 2003 (FOR-2003-11-14-1342). In Norwegian: ‘Forskrift til lov om felles regler
for det indre marked for naturgass (naturgassforskriften)’.
8 Documents No. 1488105, 1488106 and 1488107.
9 EFTA Surveillance Authority Decision No. 025/25/COL of 19 March 2025 on the temporary derogation
for the Norwegian gas distribution system operator Lyse Neo AS from requirements under Directive
2009/73/EC of 13 July 2009 concerning common rules for the internal market in natural gas.
10 RME was required to: (i) clarify its reasons for granting a continued derogation for Lyse Neo from 3
October 2024 until 31 December 2025, taking into account the elements identified by the Authority as
lacking and specified in the Authority’s decision, and (ii) justify the duration of the derogation.
11 Documents No. 1532572 and 1532614.

Page 3
2 PROCEDURE
(10)On 4 July 2025, RME submitted to the Authority a preliminary decision indicating that it
planned to continue Lyse Neo’s derogation from third-party access requirements for a
new period of five years starting from 1 January 2026 (Documents No. 1548248, 1548249
and 1548250).12 RME also submitted a supporting document (Document No. 1548247).13
(11)RME submitted additional documents, which were taken into account for the preparation
of RME’s preliminary decision, to the Authority on 15 August 202514 and on 1 October
2025.15
(12)On 3 November 2025, RME notified to the Authority the RME Decision (Documents No.
1571348, 1571349 and 1571350), maintaining its preliminary position to continue Lyse
Neo’s derogation for five years from 1 January 2026.
(13)By email of 5 November 2025, the Authority invited RME to share the input which RME
had received during the public consultation on its preliminary decision (Document No.
1572006). RME submitted that input on 7 November 2025.16
(14)Several exchanges and meetings took place between the Authority and RME to clarify
the information and documents submitted by RME.17
(15)By letters of 2 and 5 December 2025, the Authority and RME agreed to extend the period
for the Authority to issue its decision until 27 February 2026 (Documents No. 1576425
and 1579061).
3 THE NATURAL GAS MARKET IN NORWAY
(16)The Norwegian domestic market for natural gas consists of two distribution systems,
respectively owned and operated by Molgas Energy, previously known as Gasnor,
(‘Molgas’) and Lyse Neo.
3.1 Lyse Neo’s gas distribution system
(17)Lyse Neo’s gas distribution system is located in Jæren and Ryfylke. This area is subject
to the present Decision.
12 Pursuant to Section 5-2, fourth paragraph of the Norwegian regulation on natural gas no. 1342 of 14
November 2003 (FOR-2003-11-14-1342). The Authority notes that the Norwegian legal framework does
not adequately reflect the procedure for Norway under Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive, as adapted by
JCD No. 93/2017. The present Decision is without prejudice to any future action the Authority may
undertake with regard to Norway’s implementation of this procedure.
13 This supporting document sets out RME’s comments related to the following matters which were not
addressed in its preliminary decision: (i) Oslo Economics' report of February 2025, (ii) explanation of
various calculations made by RME, and (iii) clarification on the decarbonisation package.
14 Report from Menon Economics of 28 February 2025 (‘Menon Economics Report’), ‘Konsekvenser av
tredjepartsadgang for biogassprodusenter og balanseringskostnader i Lyse Neo sitt gassrørnett’
(Documents No. 1554530 and 1554532).
15 Submissions to RME by Lyse Neo, including internal accounts, product accounts, yearly costs and
revenues, and tariff estimates, as well as RME calculations. Documents No. 1565503, 1565515,
1565516, 1565517, 1565518, 1565519, 1565520, 1565521, 1565522, 1565523 and 1565524.
16 Documents No. 1572525, 1572530, 1572529, 1572528, 1572527 and 1572526.
17 The Authority and RME met on 9 May 2025, 23 June 2025, 14 August 2025, 15 September 2025 and
on 8 October 2025.

Page 4
(18)According to the RME Decision, Lyse Neo is a company part of the Lyse Group, which
has activities in, inter alia, the production of renewable power, telecommunications and
distribution of electricity. Its gas distribution system was built between 2001 and 2005 and
consists of the following infrastructure:
(i) the Rogass sea pipeline of 50 km, which mainly supplies an LNG factory in Risavika,
and which has the capacity to transport 15 TWh a year; and
(ii) 600 km of land network, with a transport capacity of 2.3 TWh a year.
(19)Since the incorporation of the Gas Directive into the EEA Agreement, Lyse Neo’s
distribution system has been exempted from Articles 24, 31 and 32 of the Gas Directive,
pursuant to Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive, as adapted by JCD No. 93/2017. According
to the information provided by RME, this exemption was granted because Lyse Neo’s
system was built during the period when Norway held the status of an “emergent market”
under Directive 2003/55/EC18 from 10 April 2004 until 10 April 2014.19
(20)Following RME’s decisions of 3 October 2024 and 22 April 2025,20 and the RME Decision
subject to the present Decision, Lyse Neo currently benefits from a derogation from third-
party access requirements under Article 32 of the Gas Directive.
3.2 Molgas’ gas distribution system
(21)Molgas’ gas distribution system, located at Karmøy in Norway and built in 1994, was
opened for third-party access on 1 January 2023. The Authority understands, based on
the information provided by RME, that no third parties currently supply gas through
Molgas’ system.
4 LEGAL FRAMEWORK
(22)Article 49(4) of the Gas Directive allows a temporary derogation from, inter alia, third-party
access requirements under Article 32 of the Gas Directive where the implementation of
the Gas Directive would cause “substantial problems” in a geographically limited area, “in
particular concerning the development of the transmission and major distribution
infrastructure, and with a view to encouraging investments […] for developments within
that area”.
(23)Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive, as adapted by JCD No. 93/2017, sets out the procedure
to be applied for Norway for the continuation of such derogations (see paragraphs (5) and
(6) above), and it provides the criteria that RME must take into account for its decision,
“in particular […]:
18 Directive 2003/55/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 26 June 2003 concerning
common rules for the internal market in natural gas and repealing Directive 98/30/EC, OJ L 176,
15.7.2003, p. 57, as incorporated into the EEA Agreement and adapted by EEA Joint Committee
Decision No. 146/2005 of 2 December 2005.
19 Pursuant to Article 2(31) of Directive 2003/55/EC, "emergent market" means an EEA State in which
the first commercial supply of its first long-term natural gas supply contract was made not more than 10
years earlier.
20 RME did not continue the derogation from the requirement to designate Lyse Neo as a DSO under
Article 24 of the Gas Directive, since Lyse Neo was designated as a DSO from 1 February 2021, and
found that it was not necessary to continue the derogation from accounting rules under Article 31 of the
Gas Directive.

Page 5
- the need for infrastructure investments, which would not be economic to operate in a
competitive market environment,
- the level and pay-back prospects of investments required,
- the size and maturity of the gas system in the area concerned,
- the prospects for the gas market concerned,
- the geographical size and characteristics of the area or region concerned, and
socioeconomic and demographic factors”.
5 THE AUTHORITY’S ASSESSMENT
5.1 Summary of the RME Decision
(24)The RME Decision continued Lyse Neo’s derogation from the third-party access
requirements under Article 32 of the Gas Directive for five years starting from 1 January
2026. RME considered that third-party access is likely to create substantial problems for
Lyse Neo’s continued operation of the gas distribution system for the following main
reasons:
- Lyse Neo has not yet recovered the historic investments it made to establish the
distribution system, and it is uncertain whether this will happen in the future given the
current market outlook;
- it is uncertain whether future investments aimed at extending the economic lifetime of
the system by transitioning to biogas are profitable; and
- Lyse Neo’s distribution system is not connected to a transmission system.
(25)With regard to the duration of the derogation, RME noted that the factual circumstances
underlying its decision are expected to persist for the period from 2026 to 2030, and that
a five-year extension would help reduce regulatory uncertainty compared to a shorter
exemption.
(26)The RME Decision is based on information provided by Lyse Neo, a consultant report,21
and stakeholders’ input.22
(27)The Authority understands that the RME Decision assessed the criteria set out in Article
49(5) of the Gas Directive as summarised in the following.
5.1.1 The need for infrastructure investments
(28)The RME Decision distinguished between investments needed independently of third-
party access and investments needed as a result of potential third-party access.
(29)Regarding the first category of investments, RME noted that Lyse Neo:
21 Menon Economics Report.
22 On 4 July 2025, RME issued a notice of exemption for third-party access to Lyse Neo's gas distribution
system in Jæren and Ryfylke with a deadline for comments by 15 August 2025. RME received in total
5 responses: four from biogas producers and one from an interest organisation of which three of the
biogas producers are members. RME also received input from several stakeholders in relation to its
decision of 3 October 2024, which it still considered as relevant.

Page 6
- is facing investments in gas meters due to the shutdown of the 2G network, estimated
at 12.2 million NOK, and potentially also in storage capacity to ensure the possibility
of balancing the distribution system if the sales volume continues to fall;
- plans to replace all natural gas in the system with biogas in the long term, which will
require further investments in storage capacity for balancing purposes; and
- presented three different scenarios for the investments needed for storage capacity
for scaling up biogas production, with estimates ranging from 55 million NOK to 1 220
million NOK.
(30)Regarding the second category of investments, RME noted that:
- facilitating third-party access, according to Lyse Neo, will require additional
investments to replace gas meters, estimated at 14.4 million NOK;23 and
- investments for adjustment in the systems for settlement and invoicing will be
required, estimated at 10 million NOK.
5.1.2 The level and pay-back prospects of investments required
(31)RME noted that the high prices for natural gas in recent years led to a loss of customers
and decline in sales volumes, reducing the operating profit for Lyse Neo.24 Therefore,
RME considered that there is great uncertainty associated with the economic lifetime of
Lyse Neo’s distribution system, making it difficult for Lyse Neo to recover both historic and
future investments, even with the derogation from third-party access requirements.
(32)With regard to the historic investments made to establish the distribution system, RME
noted that:
- Lyse Neo has not yet fully recovered its investments of 1 116.6 million NOK made to
establish the distribution system;25 and
- it is uncertain based on the current market outlook whether it is possible for Lyse Neo
to obtain a reasonable compensation for the tied-up capital associated with the historic
investments.26
(33)With regard to future investments, RME noted that:
- the profitability depends on whether and when Lyse Neo’s system is opened for third-
party access;
- opening the distribution system for third-party access would reduce profitability and
could hinder investments that may be necessary to extend the economic lifetime of
the distribution system;
- although Lyse Neo could pass these costs on to third-party actors via the tariff, the
tariff for third-party undertakings will be higher than the tariff determined for Molgas
and it is therefore doubtful whether other undertakings will be interested in supplying
gas through Lyse Neo’s system;
23 Because third-party access requires remote reading for all customers to calculate what the individual
supplier delivers to different customers over hours and/or days.
24 Because the costs of operating the grid have to be distributed among fewer customers / lower
volumes. In the period 2021-2024, Lyse Neo experienced a 50 per cent reduction in the average
contribution compared with the period 2013-2016. Its operating profit was reduced considerably over
the period, from 56 million NOK on average in the period 2013-2016 to -6 million NOK in 2021-2024.
25 Based on new figures from Lyse Neo for the period 2003-2024.
26 Based on figures for turnover, operating costs, depreciation and investments in the period 2003-2024,
RME found that the net present value of the total investments in the gas pipeline network up to 2024
has been NOK -208.1 million.

Page 7
- no third-party undertakings have yet requested access to Molgas' system.
(34)RME concluded that, if Lyse Neo’s distribution system is opened for third-party access,
there is a risk of incurring additional costs for Lyse Neo (and indirectly also for its
customers) to facilitate third-party access, without having a positive effect on the
competitive situation in the distribution system.
5.1.3 The size and maturity of the gas system in the area concerned
(35)RME noted that granting third-party access to Lyse Neo’s gas distribution system would
lead to significant difficulties with regard to balancing, given that this system is not
connected to a transmission system. In this context, Lyse Neo as a DSO has the sole
responsibility for balancing the system.
5.1.4 The prospects for the gas market concerned
(36)RME noted that:
- the sales volumes of natural gas in recent years have declined,27 due to recent high
prices of natural gas, resulting in customers reducing their energy consumption and /
or converting to alternative forms of energy, including propane and electricity; and that
- the market outlook is uncertain, both with regard to the price of natural gas and of
other forms of energy as well as regulatory factors.28
(37)With regard to the market for biogas, RME further noted that:
- it is immature and not yet competitive with other forms of energy on market terms;
- although Norway has a number of support schemes to promote biogas production,
regulatory and raw market supply chains remain uncertain;
- few customers, including the Lyse Group itself, buy biogas in Lyse Neo’s distribution
system;
- Lyse Neo seeks to replace all natural gas in the system with biogas in the long term,
and considers that third-party access plays an important role in this regard; and that
- several biogas companies with investment plans in the area may be interested in
supplying biogas through Lyse Neo’s distribution system.
5.1.5 The geographical size and characteristics of the area concerned, and socioeconomic
and demographic factors
(38)RME noted that the following features reinforce Lyse Neo’s difficulties in recovering
investments with third-party access:
- Lyse Neo’s distribution system is geographically small compared to gas systems in
EU Member States, but relatively large in relation to transported volume, which
reduces Lyse Neo’s operating profit; and that
- Lyse Neo’s customers are price-sensitive and may easily switch to alternative energy
sources such as propane and electricity.
5.1.6 Other elements
27 In the period 2017-2024, Lyse Neo experienced a 40 per cent fall in sales volumes.
28 Including the CO tax, which RME considered is likely to further increase, and regulations limiting the
2
use of natural gas.

Page 8
(39)In addition to the above criteria set out in Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive, RME
considered subsequent legal developments in the EU, in particular Directive 2024/1788
of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024 on common rules for the
internal markets in renewable gas, natural gas and hydrogen (‘Directive 2024/1788’).29
(40)RME noted that Directive 2024/1788 allows EU Member States to exempt gas distribution
systems that are not connected to other countries from third-party access requirements,
reflecting the challenges of such access for isolated systems, such as Lyse Neo’s
distribution system which is not connected to a transmission system.
5.1.7 Stakeholders’ input
(41)Finally, the RME Decision summarised the stakeholders’ input received during the public
consultation on RME’s preliminary decision, including the stakeholders’ input received in
relation to RME’s previous decision of 3 October 2024. The majority of these stakeholders
did not support the continuation of Lyse Neo’s derogation. They argued that:
- RME placed excessive emphasis on the recovery of historic investments;
- RME adopted an overly pessimistic market outlook;
- RME overstated the challenges and costs of balancing;
- the absence of third-party access would hamper the development of the biogas
industry in the area; and that
- the Norwegian regulation on natural gas30 does not permit consideration of whether
historic investments have been recovered when assessing the need for a continued
derogation, thus the legal requirements were not met.
(42)Regarding the relevance of historic investments, the RME Decision considered that:
- while return on Lyse Neo’s original investment is not explicitly listed among the
relevant criteria in the Norwegian regulation on natural gas, the reference therein to
“substantial problems […] for the development of and investment in the system”31 and
the purpose of the derogation to ensure a reasonable return for the regulated natural
monopoly allows for an assessment of whether Lyse Neo can recover the investments
necessary to establish the gas distribution system; and that
- failing to assess historic investments could undermine investment incentives.
5.2 Assessment of the legal requirements
(43)At the outset, the Authority notes that granting a derogation, being an exemption from the
rules of the Gas Directive, must be limited to what is necessary for achieving the objective
pursued, and its duration must be as short as possible. This is supported by settled case
law, from which it follows that exceptions are to be interpreted strictly so that general rules
are not negated.32
29 OJ L, 2024/1788, 15.7.2024, not yet incorporated into the EEA Agreement.
30 Section 5-2, third paragraph.
31 The Authority’s translation of Section 5-2, third paragraph, of the Norwegian regulation on natural
gas, which in Norwegian reads: ‘betydelige problemer, særlig for utvikling av og investeringer i
systemet’.
32 Compare judgment of the Court of Justice of 28 October 2022, Generalstaatsanwaltschaft München,
C-435/22 PPU, EU:C:2022:852, para. 120 and the case law cited.

Page 9
(44)The Authority, stressing that the burden to adequately demonstrate that Lyse Neo fulfils
the requirements for a continued derogation is upon RME, has the following remarks.
5.2.1 Assessment of the requirements in Article 49(4) of the Gas Directive
(45)The Authority observes that the purpose of the derogation under Article 49(4) of the Gas
Directive is to avoid “substantial problems” in a geographically limited area which may be
caused by the implementation of the Gas Directive, “in particular concerning the
development of the transmission and major distribution infrastructure, and with a view to
encouraging investments […] for developments within that area”. For example, a
derogation from third-party access requirements may encourage investments and thus
the development of infrastructure, in accordance with Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive.
(46)The Authority notes that the RME Decision concluded that opening the gas distribution
system to third-party access would likely create substantial problems for Lyse Neo’s
continued operation of the distribution system and, as such, access would reduce the
profitability of Lyse Neo’s investments which, in turn, may affect whether the investments
outlined by Lyse Neo are carried out. The Authority further understands that RME
considered that Lyse Neo's profitability in relation to both historical and future investments
must be prioritised over opening the market.
(47)While Articles 49(4) and 49(5) of the Gas Directive, read in conjunction with JCD No.
93/2017, leave discretion to RME with regards to the assessment of whether third-party
access pursuant to Article 32 of that Directive would cause ‘substantial problems’ in Jæren
and Ryfylke, the Authority observes, concerning the relevance of the historic investments
made by Lyse Neo when the distribution system was established, that:
- the requirements for granting a derogation under Articles 49(4) and 49(5) of the Gas
Directive appears to be forward-looking in nature and do not explicitly refer to such
historic investments;
- no specific adaptations to these requirements were made for Norway by JCD No.
93/2017 when granting the initial derogation for Lyse Neo’s gas distribution system,
which was already established at the time of adoption of JCD No. 93/2017; and that
- the criteria listed in Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive are not exhaustive.
(48)The Authority notes, however, that the RME Decision lacks clarity regarding RME’s
consideration of some of the criteria listed in Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive and their
implication for the assessment as to whether third-party access would cause substantial
problems as required under Article 49(4) of that Directive, as further detailed in Section
5.2.2 below.
5.2.2 Assessment of the criteria listed in Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive
(49)Firstly, with regard to “the need for infrastructure investments” and whether these “would
not be economic to operate in a competitive market environment”, in accordance with the
first criterion listed in Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive (the Authority’s emphasis), RME
noted several uncertainties as regards market and regulatory developments. The
Authority notes, however, that the RME Decision does not clearly explain whether RME
considers the investments outlined by Lyse Neo33 to be needed in light of the market
outlook.
33 The RME Decision uses different wording in this respect, referring to Lyse Neo as both “investigating”
and “planning to” undertake certain investments, which raises uncertainty as to whether those

Page 10
(50)Secondly, the Authority considers that the RME Decision does not clearly explain whether
Lyse Neo would be able to recover its investment costs through tariffs in case of third-
party access, with reference to “the level and pay-back prospects of investments required”
in the second criteria listed in Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive (the Authority’s emphasis).
The Authority notes, in this regard, that regulated tariffs allow a margin to recover costs
and that third-party access does not necessarily exclude profitability.
(51)While RME considered that it would be less profitable for Lyse Neo to undertake the
relevant investments if the distribution system were opened for third-party access, it did
not substantiate these considerations. The Authority further notes that RME considered it
doubtful whether other actors would be interested in supplying gas through Lyse Neo’s
system, inter alia because the tariffs estimated for Lyse Neo would be higher than those
for Molgas. However, in light of the number of stakeholders (e.g., biogas producers) who
expressed an interest in accessing Lyse Neo’s distribution system during the public
consultation on RME’s preliminary decision, the RME Decision does not fully explain
these considerations. The RME Decision also lacks internal consistency: if no, or only a
limited number of, third-party actors are expected to seek access, this assumption should
be reflected in RME’s assessment of the investment needs and pay-back prospects.
(52)Further to the above, the Authority notes that the RME Decision does not fully address
the elements highlighted by the Authority as lacking in its Decision No. 025/25/COL. Given
RME’s commitment to address these, inter alia through commissioning a consultant’s
report,34 the Authority invites RME to reflect, where relevant, its findings in the RME
Decision to clarify its considerations and to enhance transparency.
(53)Finally, regarding the duration of the continued derogation, the Authority notes that RME
expects the factual circumstances underlying its decision to persist for the period from
2026 to 2030. In light of the comments above, RME is invited to update, as relevant, its
assessment of the length of the derogation, noting that the derogation must be limited to
what is necessary for achieving the objective pursued, which entails that the duration must
be as short as possible (see paragraph (43) above).
(54)Due to the lack of clarity on the points listed above, the Authority considers it necessary
that RME clarifies its considerations for granting Lyse Neo a continued derogation.
5.2.3 Other elements
(55)With regard to the consideration in the RME Decision of Directive 2024/1788, the Authority
acknowledges the rationale underlying the derogation, namely, the challenges associated
with third-party access for non-connected systems, and its relevance for RME’s
assessment. However, the Authority notes that Directive 2024/1788 is not yet
incorporated into the EEA Agreement. The present Decision is based exclusively on the
requirements of the Gas Directive as currently applicable and in force under the EEA
Agreement.
5.2.4 Stakeholders’ input
investments are considered “required” and will be undertaken. In addition, it does not specify whether,
and if so which, of the estimates presented by Lyse Neo are considered the most realistic.
34 In its amended decision of 22 April 2024, RME acknowledged areas of uncertainty and indicated that
those elements would be subject to further investigations before the adoption by RME of a decision for
the period from 1 January 2026 onwards. RME therefore commissioned the Menon Economics Report.

Page 11
(56)The Authority notes that the RME Decision did not fully address the concerns raised by
stakeholders opposing the continuation of Lyse Neo’s derogation. Further to its comments
above in Section 5.2.2, and with a view to enhancing transparency, the Authority invites
RME to clarify its considerations, including, where relevant, by addressing stakeholders’
concerns.
6 CONCLUSIONS
(57)For the reasons set out above, the Authority considers that the amendments detailed in
Section 5.2.2 are necessary to ensure that the derogation granted to Lyse Neo is in line
with the requirements and the objectives of the Gas Directive.
(58)In accordance with the procedure for Norway under Article 49(5) of the Gas Directive, as
adapted by JCD No. 93/2017, the Authority requires RME to amend the RME Decision
within one month following receipt of this Decision, and to inform the Authority accordingly.
HAS ADOPTED THIS DECISION:
1. This Decision is addressed to RME.
2. This Decision enters into force upon notification to RME.
3. RME is to comply with this Decision within a period of one month and to inform the
EEA Joint Committee and the EFTA Surveillance Authority accordingly.
4. This Decision is authentic in the English language.
Done at Brussels,
For the EFTA Surveillance Authority
